behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is handler orchestration: signature verification plus queue handoff suppression. A pure Unit test of only the HMAC function would miss the 'not forwarded' side effect, so Unit is too low (MOCK-MISS). I considered System because this is an HTTP handler, but the behavior text only requires status code + no enqueue and does not require real socket/network behavior, so in-process module wiring disqualifies System (REJECTION-GATE). No AI/ML output or multi-agent sequence is involved, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the real webhook handler with real signature-verification middleware/function and a spy/fake queue module. Send a crafted request object with raw body bytes and an intentionally incorrect `X-Signature-256`. Assert `401`, and assert queue `enqueue` was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee between handler and queue persistence call across module boundaries. Unit is too low because it can validate control flow but not the real handoff to the persistence-backed queue adapter (MOCK-MISS). I considered System due HTTP response timing, but the behavior text is specifically about internal ordering ('written ... before ... 200') and does not require real network/OS effects, so System is disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Use the real handler and real queue adapter against a local persistent store (e.g., sqlite/file). Instrument queue write completion (or block commit until signaled). Assert the response is not completed before queue write commit; then release commit and assert `200` is returned.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: The bug appears at module interaction between request handler, queue publish, and async consumer trigger. Unit is too low because this is not just one function's algorithm; it is decoupling behavior across modules (MOCK-MISS). I considered System, but behavior text targets 'without waiting for downstream processing' rather than real network/OS conditions, so in-process integration is sufficient and System is disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler + queue + downstream worker in-process. Make downstream worker intentionally slow (e.g., deferred promise). Send valid webhook and assert HTTP response completes quickly while worker promise is still pending; then allow worker to finish.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is explicitly wall-clock latency under concurrency, which depends on real scheduling/timing and HTTP serving behavior (REAL-INFRA). In-process mocks/fake timers can hide queue contention, event-loop starvation, and real request handling delays (MOCK-HIDE). I considered Integration but rejected it because the behavior text requires 'within 3 seconds wall-clock time' under concurrent load, which is a System boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real Node HTTP service on an ephemeral port with real timer clock. Preload slow/concurrent downstream work from earlier events, then send concurrent valid webhook requests over real HTTP. Measure end-to-end response time per request and assert each acknowledgment arrives < 3 seconds.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Deduplication is a state handoff behavior across handler and persistent queue/idempotency store, not a single-function check (MOCK-MISS). Unit tests of pure ID comparison can pass while integration fails due persistence/race wiring. I considered System, but the behavior text does not require real network/OS/subprocess behavior; local persistence wiring is enough, so System is disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Use handler + real persistent queue/idempotency storage locally. Submit the same event ID twice (including near-concurrent submission). Assert only one queue record exists for that ID and second submission does not enqueue another record.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This is error propagation/mapping across module boundary: queue-full signal -> HTTP contract. Unit is too low because it can miss actual wiring between queue error type and handler response construction (MOCK-MISS). I considered System, but behavior text starts from 'queue reports it is full' and requires response mapping only; no real OS/network condition is required, so System is disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Integrate handler with queue module configured to return a concrete `QueueFull` failure. Send valid webhook and assert HTTP `503` plus `Retry-After` header is present; assert no downstream processing starts.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is queue logic plus its persistence backend contract (local DB/filesystem), which is beyond a single pure function. Unit can miss storage-backed enforcement behavior and transaction semantics (MOCK-MISS). I considered System, but behavior text does not require external network/service/OS primitives beyond local persistence; Integration with local persistent backend disqualifies System.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue against its local storage backend with capacity `N`. Enqueue `N` events successfully, then enqueue `N+1` and assert deterministic rejection. Verify persisted state reflects exactly `N` accepted items and no overflow write occurred.
